name: Geri
description: Geri, meaning 'greedy' or 'ravenous,' is one of the two wolves who accompany the god Odin in Norse mythology. He is mentioned in the Poetic Edda, compiled in the 13th century from earlier traditional sources, and the Prose Edda, written in the 13th century by Snorri Sturluson. Geri and his brother Freki are Odin's loyal companions, feasting on the meat placed before Odin at Valhalla. Odin himself consumes only wine, dedicating the food to his wolves. Their presence highlights Odin's role as a war god and a leader of warriors, as wolves were often associated with battle and the scavenging of corpses on the battlefield. Geri and Freki symbolize Odin's power and connection to the wild, untamed aspects of nature. While the exact nature of their powers or specific roles beyond companionship are not explicitly detailed, their constant presence alongside Odin underscores their significance within his retinue. They embody loyalty, ferocity, and the inherent wildness associated with the god of wisdom, war, and death. Geri’s name itself, signifying greed or ravenousness, further emphasizes the predatory nature often attributed to wolves and their connection to the battlefields of Norse lore. They are integral parts of Odin's iconography, representing his multifaceted nature and his dominion over both the civilized and untamed realms.
subtitle: Odin's Loyal Wolf Companion

keyMyths.title: Odin's Wolves
keyMyths.description: Geri and Freki accompany Odin, feasting on the food provided in Valhalla while Odin drinks only wine.

keyMyths.title: Symbolism of Wolves
keyMyths.description: Wolves are symbols of war and the battlefield, reflecting Odin's role as a war god and leader of warriors.

keyMyths.title: Odin's Companions
keyMyths.description: Geri and Freki remain by Odin's side, representing his connection to the wild and untamed aspects of nature.

sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems, including Grímnismál.

cultural.modernLegacy: While Geri doesn't have a direct modern cult following, he represents the loyal animal companion archetype. He and Freki are often referenced in modern pagan and Heathenry contexts when discussing Odin and his retinue, and serve as symbols of loyalty and the wild, untamed aspects of nature. They also appear in modern fiction, games, and art inspired by Norse mythology, representing Odin's power and connection to the animal world.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Geri are somewhat shrouded in mystery, as Norse mythology doesn't explicitly detail his birth or parentage. He simply appears as one of Odin's two wolf companions, alongside Freki. The name 'Geri' itself, meaning 'greedy' or 'ravenous,' hints at his nature and role. It is believed that Geri and Freki are manifestations of Odin's power and his connection to the animal world, embodying the fierce, predatory aspects of the Allfather. Some scholars speculate that they may be ancient spirit animals or totems associated with Odin's cult, predating the more formalized mythology recorded in the Eddas. Their presence reinforces Odin's image as a powerful, shamanistic figure with dominion over both the human and animal realms.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Geri's role in Norse mythology is primarily defined by his constant companionship with Odin. The most prominent mention of Geri and Freki appears in the Poetic Edda (Grímnismál, stanza 19) where Odin states that he gives all the food placed before him to his wolves, as he himself needs no sustenance other than mead. This highlights Odin's spiritual nature and his focus on wisdom and magic rather than physical needs. Geri and Freki are always by Odin's side, whether in Valhalla or on the battlefield. While they do not have individual stories attributed to them, their presence serves as a constant reminder of Odin's power, his connection to the wild, and the loyalty he inspires. Their role is more symbolic than narrative, representing the predatory and instinctual aspects of Odin's character.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: There is no direct evidence of a specific cult or dedicated worship of Geri in historical Norse paganism. While Odin was widely venerated, his animal companions were likely seen as extensions of his power rather than deities in their own right. It is possible that offerings were made to Odin with the implicit understanding that his wolves would benefit, but there are no surviving accounts detailing specific rituals or prayers directed towards Geri. The focus of Norse religious practice was generally on the major gods and goddesses, with animal symbolism playing a more indirect role in expressing the qualities and attributes of those deities. Geri and Freki, therefore, were likely revered as symbols of Odin's might and his connection to the animal world, rather than as independent entities deserving of their own cult following.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Geri's legacy extends beyond his direct role in Norse mythology. He and Freki have become enduring symbols of loyalty, companionship, and the connection between humans and animals. They frequently appear in modern interpretations of Norse mythology, from literature and art to video games and popular culture. They represent the wild, untamed aspects of nature and the powerful bond between a god and his animal familiars. In modern Heathenry and Norse paganism, Geri and Freki serve as reminders of Odin's multifaceted nature and the importance of honoring the animal world. They symbolize the loyalty and ferocity that Odin values in his warriors and followers, and their image continues to inspire those who seek to connect with the ancient Norse traditions.

shortDescription: Geri is one of Odin's two wolves in Norse mythology. Meaning 'greedy', he and Freki feast alongside the Allfather in Valhalla.
